headline: Bannack Founded, 1862
body:
Well now, if you ever heard tell of a place that sprung up faster than a jackrabbit on a hot griddle, you’d be talking about Bannack, Montana. It was the year of our Lord 1862, and the good Lord, or perhaps the devil himself, saw fit to sprinkle a bit of gold dust in Grasshopper Creek. John White, a man whose name sounds as plain as a fence post but whose discovery was anything but, stumbled upon the glittering stuff on July 28th. And just like that, the quiet hills of what was then Idaho Territory, soon to be Montana, erupted into a frenzy. 

Folks came pouring in, a motley collection of dreamers and desperadoes, honest men and those whose honesty was as thin as a politician’s promise. They came from every corner, lured by the siren song of easy riches. Where there was nothing but sagebrush and silence one day, there was a bustling, bawling, brawling town the next. By 1863, Bannack’s population swelled to over three thousand souls, each with a pickaxe in one hand and a prayer, or a pistol, in the other. The main street, a muddy track carved by wagons and ambition, soon boasted three hotels, three bakeries, three blacksmith shops, two stables, two meat markets, a grocery store, a restaurant, a brewery, a billiard hall, and no less than four saloons. Every structure, mind you, was built of logs, though some put on airs with fancy false fronts, trying to look more respectable than the wild spirit that animated them.

Among this throng was a Dr. Erasmus Darwin Leavitt, a man of medicine from Cornish, New Hampshire, who, like so many, traded his lancet for a shovel. He arrived in Bannack in ’62, practicing his healing arts one day and digging for gold the next. It wasn’t long, however, before he found, as the historian Joaquin Miller put it, that “he had more reputation as a physician than as a miner, and that there was greater profit in allowing someone else to wield his pick and shovel while he attended to his profession.” A sensible man, that Dr. Leavitt, who eventually moved on to Butte, leaving the rough-and-tumble of Bannack behind.

But where there’s gold, there’s greed, and where there’s greed, there’s often a shortage of law. Bannack, bless its wild heart, had more than its share of both. The roads leading to the gold fields became hunting grounds for a breed of men who preferred to relieve miners of their hard-won dust with a bullet rather than a pan. And at the head of this dubious enterprise, or so many believed, was none other than the town’s own sheriff, Henry Plummer. A man of considerable charm and cunning, Plummer cut a fine figure, with a polished address and a knack for understanding his fellow man. Nathaniel Pitt Langford, a man who knew a thing or two about vigilantes, once wrote of Plummer, “He possessed great executive ability, a power over men that was remarkable, a fine person, polished address, and prescient knowledge of his fellows—all of which were mellowed by the advantages of a good early education.” A man of such talents, one might think, destined for greatness. Instead, he was destined for a rope.

The air in Bannack was thick with suspicion. Men walked armed, and every shadow seemed to hold a threat. Shootings were as common as dust storms, and the little graveyard on the hill filled up with alarming speed, its tenants mostly having met a violent end. It was a carnival of crime, where the honest folk lived in constant dread, and the law, such as it was, seemed to wink at the villains. The climax of this lawlessness came on January 10, 1864, when Plummer and two of his deputies were strung up by the neck, without so much as a proper trial, by a group of determined citizens who had finally had enough. The Vigilance Committee, as they called themselves, took matters into their own hands, and in doing so, they brought a brutal end to a brutal era. Bannack, for all its gold and glory, was a town built on a foundation of raw ambition and desperate measures, a place where fortunes were made and lost, and where the line between lawman and outlaw was often as blurry as a distant mountain in a summer haze.
